attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a three-day history of right breast pain and redness. She gave birth to her first child four weeks ago and has been breastfeeding. She has been having difficulty with latch and has been using a pump to assist with feeding. She has not seen a lactation consultant. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 38.5°C. The right breast is tender, warm, and erythematous, particularly in the upper outer quadrant. There is a 2 cm fluctuant area felt which is exquisitely tender.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms suggestive of a breast abscess, which is a complication of mastitis. The key features supporting this diagnosis are the presence of a fluctuant mass, severe tenderness, and systemic symptoms (fever, tachycardia) in a breastfeeding woman.

Mastitis is indeed a common condition in breastfeeding women and often precedes abscess formation. However, the presence of a fluctuant mass suggests that the condition has progressed beyond simple mastitis to abscess formation.

Inflammatory breast cancer can present with breast erythema and swelling but is typically not associated with a discrete fluctuant mass. It is also rare, especially in young, lactating women.

A breast accessory duct is a congenital anomaly that can become infected, but it would not typically present with such an acute onset or systemic symptoms.

A blocked duct can cause localised tenderness and swelling but usually does not lead to systemic symptoms or a fluctuant mass unless it progresses to mastitis and then to an abscess.

The management of a breast abscess typically involves ultrasound-guided aspiration or incision and drainage, along with antibiotic therapy. Continued breastfeeding or expression of milk from the affected breast is usually recommended to prevent milk stasis. If the abscess is large or recurrent, surgical drainage may be necessary. Follow-up is important to ensure resolution and to prevent recurrence.

Ultrasound is the preferred imaging modality for diagnosing breast abscesses in lactating women. It can confirm the presence of an abscess, guide aspiration or drainage procedures, and help differentiate between abscesses and other breast masses. In would also be appropriate to order additional blood tests such as a complete blood count and C-reactive protein to assess the severity of the infection and guide treatment decisions.
learningPoints: Breast abscess is a complication of mastitis, typically presenting with a fluctuant mass, severe tenderness, and systemic symptoms.
Ultrasound is the preferred imaging modality for diagnosing breast abscesses in lactating women.
Breast abscesses require drainage in addition to antibiotic therapy.
references: Ulitzsch D, Nyman MK, Carlson RA. Breast abscess in lactating women: US-guided treatment. Radiology. 2004 Sep;232(3):904-9. doi: 10.1148/radiol.2323030582. Epub 2004 Jul 29. PMID: 15284435.

Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP). Lactational mastitis and breast abscess [Internet]. East Melbourne: RACGP; 2022. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/ccf54e89-7e44-409a-afa9-c14f1bb40639/Lactational-mastitis-and-breast-abscess.aspx
